02cc57701d06a99b.summary: Perimeter/circumference of a circle = 2πr. Area of a circle = πr². These are fundamental to advanced concepts like sector area, segment area, and area of combinations of plane figures.

chunks.title: Understanding Perimeter and Area of a Circle
chunks.content:
**Perimeter and Area of a Circle** is a key concept in Areas Related to Circles of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

**Circle:** Set of points equidistant from a fixed centre.
**Perimeter (Circumference):** C = 2πr = πd
**Area:** A = πr²

π (pi) ≈ 22/7 ≈ 3.14159 — the ratio of circumference to diameter.

**Relationship:** A = C²/(4π), C = √(4πA)

**Key points to remember:**
• C = 2πr or πd (diameter = 2r)
• A = πr²
• Use π ≈ 22/7 or 3.14 as specified
• Area/Circumference ratio gives r

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Perimeter and Area of a Circle
chunks.content:
**1. Properties:**
• π is irrational; 22/7 and 3.14 are approximations
• Doubling radius quadruples area
• Semicircle: Perimeter = πr+2r, Area = πr²/2
• Quarter circle: Perimeter = πr/2+2r, Area = πr²/4

**2. Important Formulas:**
Circumference C = 2πr = πd
Area A = πr²
A = C²/(4π)
C = 2√(πA)

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
Find circ. and area of circle radius 14 cm.
C = 2×22/7×14 = 88 cm
A = 22/7×196 = 616 cm²

chunks.title: Worked Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** The circumference of a circle is 220 cm. Find its radius and area.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** C = 2πr → 220 = 2×22/7×r
**Step 2:** r = 220×7/44 = 35 cm
**Step 3:** A = πr² = 22/7×35×35 = 3850 cm²

Therefore, r = 35 cm, A = 3850 cm².

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **π≈22/7 vs 3.14:** Both are approximations; use what's specified

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **C = 2πr NOT πr²:** Don't confuse circumference with area formula

chunks.title: Practice Problems
chunks.content:
**Problem 1 (Basic):**
Find circumference and area of circle radius 21 cm.

**Answer:** C=2×22/7×21=132 cm. A=22/7×441=1386 cm².

---

**Problem 2 (Intermediate):**
Area of circle is 616 cm². Find circumference.

**Answer:** πr²=616 → r=14. C=2×22/7×14=88 cm.

---

**Problem 3 (Advanced):**
Wire bent into circle radius 14 cm. Rebent into square, find side.

**Answer:** C=2×22/7×14=88. Perim(square)=88 → side=22 cm.

89bf3724d9e8b68f.summary: Sector: arc length l = θ/360°×2πr, area = θ/360°×πr² = ½lr. Segment area = sector area - triangle area = θ/360°×πr² - ½r² sin θ. Minor/major refer to angle < or > 180°.

chunks.title: Understanding Areas of Sector and Segment of a Circle
chunks.content:
**Areas of Sector and Segment of a Circle** is a key concept in Areas Related to Circles of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

**Sector:** Region between two radii and the corresponding arc.
**Segment:** Region between a chord and its corresponding arc.

**Formulas:**
Arc length (l) = θ/360° × 2πr
Sector area = θ/360° × πr² = ½lr
Segment area = Sector area - Triangle area
= θ/360°×πr² - ½r² sin θ

**Key points to remember:**
• Minor sector: θ<180°, Major: θ>180°
• Segment = Sector - Triangle
• Arc length l, radius r → area = lr/2
• sin θ in degrees for triangle area

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Areas of Sector and Segment
chunks.content:
**1. Properties:**
• Sector area proportional to central angle
• l = rθ (if θ in radians)
• Segment area = r²(πθ/360° - sin θ/2)
• Perpendicular from centre bisects chord

**2. Important Formulas:**
Arc length l = θ/360° × 2πr
Sector area = θ/360° × πr² = ½lr
Segment area = r²(πθ/360° - ½ sin θ)

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
Chord of radius 10 cm subtends 90° at centre. Segment area = 25π-50 = 25(π-2) ≈ 28.54 cm².

chunks.title: Worked Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** A circle radius 14 cm has sector angle 60°. Find sector area and arc length.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Sector area = 60/360 × 22/7 × 196 = 1/6 × 616 = 102.67 cm²
**Step 2:** Arc length = 60/360 × 2 × 22/7 × 14 = 1/6 × 88 = 14.67 cm

Therefore, Area = 102.67 cm², Arc = 14.67 cm.

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **Sector vs segment:** Sector includes centre; segment is between chord and arc

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **Segment = Sector - Triangle:** Always subtract the triangle

chunks.title: Practice Problems
chunks.content:
**Problem 1 (Basic):**
A circle radius 7 cm has sector angle 60°. Find sector area.

**Answer:** 60/360×22/7×49=77/6≈12.83 cm².

---

**Problem 2 (Intermediate):**
A chord subtends 90° at centre of circle radius 10 cm. Find segment area.

**Answer:** Sector=25π, Triangle=50, Segment=25(π-2)≈28.54 cm².

---

**Problem 3 (Advanced):**
A round table top has 6 equal sectors, radius 90 cm. Find each sector area.

**Answer:** 60/360×π×8100=1350π≈4241.15 cm².

44640b46e4a8a223.summary: Composite area problems combine circles with other shapes. Approach: identify all component shapes, compute each area, add or subtract as needed. Common patterns: square with inscribed circle, circular paths, rectangular sheets with circular cutouts.

chunks.title: Understanding Areas of Combinations of Plane Figures
chunks.content:
**Areas of Combinations of Plane Figures** is a key concept in Areas Related to Circles of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

**Shaded Area = Area of larger shape - Area of cut-out regions**

**Common patterns:**
• Square with inscribed circle (shaded = square - circle)
• Circle with inscribed square (shaded = circle - square)
• Two semicircles on sides of a rectangle
• Concentric circular paths (washer)

**Key points to remember:**
• Identify all component shapes first
• Compute each area separately
• Add or subtract as needed
• Use π ≈ 22/7 or 3.14 as specified

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Areas of Combinations of Plane Figures
chunks.content:
**1. Properties:**
• Square - inscribed circle: s² - π(s/2)²
• Circle - inscribed square: πr² - 2r²
• Rectangular sheet with 4 quarter circles: rectangle - circle
• Concentric rings: π(R²-r²)

**2. Important Formulas:**
Shaded = Outer - Inner(s)
Concentric ring: π(R²-r²)

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
A circular pond radius 7 m has a 2 m wide path around it.
R=9, r=7. Path = π(81-49) = 32π ≈ 100.57 m².

chunks.title: Worked Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** A square metal sheet side 14 cm has 4 holes drilled (each radius 2 cm). Find remaining area.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Square area = 196 cm²
**Step 2:** 4 holes = 4 × π × 4 = 16π ≈ 50.27 cm²
**Step 3:** Remaining = 196 - 50.27 ≈ 145.73 cm²

Therefore, Remaining area ≈ 145.73 cm².

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **Wrong radius for path:** R = r + width, NOT r - width

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **Sketch first:** Draw the figure to see the components

chunks.title: Practice Problems
chunks.content:
**Problem 1 (Basic):**
A square side 28 cm has four equal circles at corners (radius 7 cm). Find remaining area.

**Answer:** Square=784. Four quarter-circles = one full circle = 154. Remaining=630 cm².

---

**Problem 2 (Intermediate):**
A 2 m wide circular track has inner radius 14 m. Find track area.

**Answer:** R=16. Area=π(256-196)=60π≈188.57 m².

---

**Problem 3 (Advanced):**
Four circles of radius 7 cm cut from a rectangle 50 cm×30 cm. Find remaining area.

**Answer:** Sheet=1500. Four circles=616. Remaining=884 cm².
